flashcards.back: A combinatorial optimization problem where each item can only be taken once (0 = not taken, 1 = taken). Given items with weights and values, find the maximum value that can be obtained within a given weight capacity.

flashcards.front: What is the recurrence relation for 0/1 Knapsack?
flashcards.back: dp[i][w] = max(dp[i-1][w], dp[i-1][w-weight[i]] + value[i]) if weight[i] ≤ w, else dp[i][w] = dp[i-1][w]

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of the standard DP approach for Knapsack?
flashcards.back: O(n × W) where n is the number of items and W is the knapsack capacity.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of the standard DP approach for Knapsack?
flashcards.back: O(n × W) for the 2D DP table, but can be optimized to O(W) using 1D array.

flashcards.front: What is memoization in the context of Knapsack?
flashcards.back: A top-down approach where recursive calls store results of subproblems in a table to avoid recomputation. Also called tabulation in bottom-up DP.

flashcards.front: Why can't Greedy approach solve 0/1 Knapsack optimally?
flashcards.back: Greedy chooses items based on highest value-to-weight ratio but doesn't consider remaining capacity and may include fractions that violate the 0/1 constraint.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between 0/1 Knapsack and Fractional Knapsack?
flashcards.back: 0/1: items must be taken entirely or not at all. Fractional: items can be taken in fractions. Fractional can be solved by Greedy, 0/1 requires DP.

flashcards.front: How do you reconstruct the selected items in Knapsack?
flashcards.back: Start from dp[n][W], if dp[n][w] ≠ dp[n-1][w], item n was selected. Move backwards checking which decisions changed the value.

flashcards.front: What is the base case in Knapsack DP?
flashcards.back: When no items (i=0) or zero capacity (w=0), dp[0][w] = 0 and dp[i][0] = 0.

flashcards.front: What is the space-optimized approach for Knapsack?
flashcards.back: Use a 1D array of size W+1, iterate capacity from W down to weight[i] to ensure each item is used only once: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight[i]] + value[i]).

flashcards.front: What is the unbounded knapsack problem?
flashcards.back: Each item can be taken multiple times (unlimited copies). Uses the recurrence: dp[w] = max(dp[w], dp[w-weight[i]] + value[i]) iterating forward.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of the recursive (naive) Knapsack?
flashcards.back: O(2^n) - exponential, as each item has two choices (take or not take), leading to 2^n possible combinations.

flashcards.front: What is the optimal substructure property?
flashcards.back: The optimal solution to the problem depends on optimal solutions to its subproblems. In Knapsack, best solution for first i items and capacity w depends on solutions for i-1 items.

flashcards.front: What is the overlapping subproblems property?
flashcards.back: The same subproblems are solved multiple times. In Knapsack, different combinations of items leading to same weight produce same subproblem results.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of finding selected items after DP?
flashcards.back: O(n + W) - we traverse back through the DP table, checking at most n rows and W columns.
